food_subgroup: Fermented rice and lentil pancakes with egg topping

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general uttapam entry; no specific egg-topped variant exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific uttapam or South Indian fermented pancake code. Generic pancake category A0CKP used as closest parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for uttapam or egg uttapam as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium egg uttapam weighing approximately 150 g, as typically served at a South Indian restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Uttapam (plain) and egg composition — derived from ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 (rice-lentil pancake base) combined with USDA FoodData Central whole egg data (FDC ID 748967), proportionally weighted for a typical egg-topped uttapam preparation.
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition) and USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Plain uttapam base values taken from ICMR-NIN 2017 Table (rice-urad dal fermented pancake). One medium egg (~50 g) added per 100 g serving proportion. Final per-100 g values computed as weighted blend: approximately 70% uttapam base and 30% whole egg contribution, reflecting typical restaurant preparation. Energy cross-checked against HPB Singapore Foodo analogous South Indian pancake entries.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of South Indian fermented foods — Idli and Dosa analogues; peer-reviewed reference: Shobana S et al. (2007) 'Glycemic index of commonly consumed cereal foods' Journal of Food Science and Technology; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for fermented rice-lentil pancake analogues.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Journal of Food Science and Technology (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for egg uttapam specifically. GI of 54 is estimated from the fermented rice-urad dal pancake base (idli/dosa family, GI range 48–62 per University of Sydney GI Database and Shobana et al. 2007). The egg topping contributes negligible glycemic load. A midpoint estimate of 54 is used, placing the dish in the low GI category. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (54 × 24.5 × 1.50) / 100 = 19.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~465 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one piece and avoid additional salt or high-sodium chutneys, as a single serving contributes approximately 20% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load with low GI (estimated GI 54); glycemic load per serving approximately 19.8 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Egg uttapam has a low estimated glycemic index (~54) due to fermentation of the rice-lentil batter, but the glycemic load per serving is moderate (~19.8 g); individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume one piece with a protein-rich accompaniment such as sambar to further blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol from egg (~95 mg per 100 g) and moderate saturated fat (~1.4 g per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The egg component contributes dietary cholesterol (~142 mg per serving); individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to one serving and pair with fibre-rich accompaniments such as sambar or vegetable chutney to support lipid management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Egg uttapam (also spelled egg uthappam or egg oothappam) is a South Indian savoury pancake made from a fermented batter of ground rice and urad dal (black gram lentil), topped with a beaten or cracked egg that is cooked directly onto the pancake surface on a hot griddle with a small amount of oil. The batter is typically fermented overnight, which gives the pancake its characteristic mild sourness, soft and slightly spongy texture, and improved digestibility. Common toppings alongside the egg include finely chopped onion, green chilli, and fresh coriander. In Singapore, egg uttapam is a popular breakfast and tiffin item served at South Indian and banana-leaf restaurants, often accompanied by coconut chutney and sambar (a lentil-based vegetable soup). The dish is nutritionally notable for its combination of complex carbohydrates from the fermented rice-lentil base, complete protein from the egg, and moderate fat. The fermentation process lowers the glycemic index relative to plain rice dishes (estimated GI ~54, low category). Clinically, the dish is suitable for most adults in moderate portions; individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes should note the moderate glycemic load per serving (~19.8 g) and pair the dish with sambar for additional fibre and protein. Sodium content is moderate and relevant for hypertension management. The egg contributes dietary cholesterol, which is a consideration for cardiovascular disease patients. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 175 kcal, 7.2 g protein, 5.8 g fat, and 24.5 g carbohydrate.
common_user_queries: How many calories in egg uttapam?
Is egg uttapam good for diabetics?
What is the GI of uttapam?

confidence_reason: No exact nutrient entry for egg uttapam exists in a single approved primary source. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN 2017 (plain uttapam base) and USDA FoodData Central (whole egg), proportionally blended. GI is an analogous estimate from the fermented rice-lentil pancake family per University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is medium rather than low because both component sources are high-quality approved primary sources and the blending methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are computed as a weighted blend of ICMR-NIN 2017 plain uttapam (70% weight) and USDA FoodData Central whole egg FDC 748967 (30% weight), reflecting a typical restaurant preparation of one medium egg per uttapam. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0; this is confirmed zero per both ICMR-NIN and USDA source data for this food type — confirmed zero. 3. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate from the fermented rice-lentil pancake family (idli/dosa range 48–62); no direct GI measurement for egg uttapam was found in approved sources. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from the weighted component blend and are not directly reported as a single entry in any approved source; these values carry medium confidence. 5. The input record classified this dish under cuisine 'Malaysian' and food_group 'Dairy and eggs'; both have been corrected — the dish is South Indian in origin (served at a South Indian restaurant in Singapore), and the food_group has been reclassified to 'Grains and staples' as the primary macronutrient base is fermented rice-lentil batter. 6. Halal status is marked false as egg uttapam may contain non-halal-certified eggs depending on the restaurant; consumers should verify with the specific establishment. 7. Serving size of 150 g reflects one medium uttapam as typically served in Singapore South Indian restaurants.
